stories.title: Korda Wins Chevron Championship — 3rd Major, Retakes World No. 1 (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
NELLY KORDA. THREE MAJORS.

Wire-to-wire. 18-under 270. Won by 5 shots. Retakes World No. 1.

Her 36-hole total of 130 was the lowest opening in LPGA major history.

She opened with back-to-back 65s and never looked back. Dominant. #LPGA #ChevronChampionship

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Nelly Korda's major resume:
→ Chevron Championship 2021
→ U.S. Women's Open 2024
→ Chevron Championship 2026

Three majors. Two Chevron titles. Wire-to-wire this week with a 36-hole LPGA major record (130).

She's in the conversation for best of her generation. #LPGA #Golf

facebook.long_form:
Window: 8:00 AM ET

Nelly Korda walked off Memorial Park Golf Club on Sunday with her third major championship — and the world No. 1 ranking back in her hands.

Korda closed with a 2-under 70 to win the 2026 Chevron Championship at 18-under 270, a five-shot victory over Yin Ruoning and Patty Tavatanakit. It was a wire-to-wire dominant performance that never really felt in doubt after the opening two rounds.

The headline stat from Korda's week: she opened with back-to-back 65s on Thursday and Friday, giving her a 36-hole total of 130 — the lowest opening 36 holes in LPGA major championship history (excluding the Amundi Evian Championship). That kind of early dominance made the final round feel more like a procession than a contest.

Korda added rounds of 70-70 over the weekend to hold on comfortably. She cashed a winner's check of $1.35 million from the $9 million purse and claimed her 17th LPGA Tour title — including her second LPGA victory of 2026.

With the victory, Korda retakes the world No. 1 ranking from Jeeno Thitikul for the first time since August 2025. Her major résumé now reads: Chevron Championship 2021, U.S. Women's Open 2024, Chevron Championship 2026.

She jumped in the lake after winning. The celebration was everything.

#GolfFanrecap | #LPGA | #ChevronChampionship

article.headline: Korda Wins Chevron Championship in Wire-to-Wire Demolition, Claims Third Major and World No. 1

stories.title: Fitzpatrick Brothers Win Zurich Classic — Dramatic 72nd Hole Finish (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Matt Fitzpatrick: bunker shot to 14 inches on the 72nd hole.

Alex: tap-in birdie.

Fitzpatrick brothers win the Zurich Classic at 31-under — by 1 shot after squandering a 4-shot lead on the back nine.

What a finish. #ZurichClassic #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Alex Fitzpatrick: in on a SPONSOR EXEMPTION.

He leaves with a 2-year PGA Tour card through 2028, spots in next 4 Signature Events + PGA Championship.

Matt's bunker shot to 14 inches at 18 changed his brother's life.

#PGATour #ZurichClassic

facebook.long_form:
Window: 1:00 PM ET

They squandered a four-shot lead. They made double bogey at the 12th hole after Matt drove right and Alex hit a tree with his punch-out attempt. They were rattled, the lead was gone, and two other teams were right behind them.

Then Matt Fitzpatrick hit one of the clutch shots of the season.

On the par-5 18th at TPC Louisiana, his brother Alex found a bunker in front of the green. Matt stepped in, blasted the bunker shot to within 14 inches of the hole, and Alex tapped in for the winning birdie. Fitzpatrick brothers win the 2026 Zurich Classic at a tournament-record 31-under 257 — one stroke over two teams: Americans Alex Smalley and Hayden Springer, and Norwegians Kristoffer Reitan and Kris Ventura.

The prize was significant: $2,745,500 as a team, with each brother taking home $1,372,750. But the bigger prize for Alex was the PGA Tour card that came with the victory — a two-year exemption through 2028 that changes his professional life entirely.

This was a tournament where the Fitzpatricks set a 54-hole scoring record (R3 was a tournament-record 15-under 57 in fourball), lost control on the back nine Sunday, and then dug deep when it mattered most.

That's why majors are decided on Sundays.

#GolfFanrecap | #PGATour | #ZurichClassic

article.headline: Fitzpatrick Brothers Win Zurich Classic on 72nd Hole Bunker Shot After Squandering Four-Shot Lead

stories.title: Alex Fitzpatrick's Cinderella PGA Tour Card Story (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 3-1
x_posts.text:
Six months ago, Alex Fitzpatrick was outside the top 280 on DP World Tour.

Sunday: won the Zurich Classic on a sponsor exemption. Left with a 2-year PGA Tour card through 2028.

Matt's bunker to 14 inches at 18 started it all. Brother stuff. #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 3-2
x_posts.text:
Alex Fitzpatrick leaves New Orleans with:
→ 2-year PGA Tour exemption
→ Spots in next 4 Signature Events
→ Berth in the PGA Championship
→ 2027 Players Championship invite

A week ago he was 7th in Race to Dubai. Today he's a PGA Tour winner.

#PGATour #ZurichClassic

facebook.long_form:
Window: 8:30 AM ET

Six months ago, Alex Fitzpatrick's professional career was in a precarious place.

The younger Fitzpatrick brother had slipped outside the top 280 on the DP World Tour by early October after six missed cuts in nine starts. He was on the verge of losing his card entirely. He fought back, climbed to seventh in the Race to Dubai standings — close enough that a top-10 finish at year's end would earn him PGA Tour status for the following season.

Then he got a sponsor exemption into the 2026 Zurich Classic of New Orleans, partnered with his older brother Matt, and played alongside one of the best golfers on the planet for four rounds at TPC Louisiana.

On Sunday, he tapped in a 14-inch putt on the 72nd hole to complete what Matt's stunning bunker shot had set up — and Alex Fitzpatrick left New Orleans as a PGA Tour winner with a two-year exemption through 2028.

The victory earns him entry into the next four Signature Events, a berth in the PGA Championship and an invitation to the 2027 Players Championship. He went from a sponsor exemption to fully exempt in the time it takes Matt Fitzpatrick to play a bunker shot.

Alex is a Wake Forest University alumnus. His older brother Matt is a former U.S. Open champion and current world No. 3. The dynamic between them — big brother providing the bunker shot of his life to change little brother's career — is one of the best golf stories of 2026.

#GolfFanrecap | #PGATour | #ZurichClassic

article.headline: From Near-Relegation to PGA Tour Card: Alex Fitzpatrick's Cinderella Story at Zurich Classic

stories.title: Korda's Path to 3 Majors — Inside a Historic Chevron Performance (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Korda opened the Chevron with back-to-back 65s — a 36-hole total of 130, the lowest opening in LPGA major history.

She added 70-70. Five-shot win. Wire-to-wire.

This wasn't a grind. It was a coronation. #LPGA #Golf #NellyKorda

facebook.long_form:
Window: 10:30 AM ET

Nelly Korda didn't just win the Chevron Championship this week. She turned it into one of the most dominant major championship performances the LPGA Tour has ever seen.

Consider the numbers: 65-65-70-70 = 18-under 270. Bogey-free in the first two rounds. A 36-hole total of 130, the lowest ever recorded in LPGA major championship history (excluding the Amundi Evian Championship). A five-shot final margin that was never in serious doubt.

Her three majors now span different chapters of her career: the 2021 Chevron Championship, the 2024 U.S. Women's Open and now the 2026 Chevron Championship again. She won the Chevron once as a rising star and has now returned five years later as one of the defining figures of women's professional golf.

The world No. 1 ranking she reclaims — for the first time since August 2025 — reflects the full picture: Korda is at the top of her game, and her week in Houston was the most convincing possible statement of that fact.

"I just really locked in," she said after the victory.

It showed.

#GolfFanrecap | #LPGA | #NellyKorda

article.headline: Three Majors, Three Chapters: Inside Nelly Korda's Historic Chevron Championship Week

stories.title: What's Next in Golf — PGA Tour, LIV Virginia, LPGA Calendar (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Weekend recap:
✅ Korda — 3rd major, World No. 1
✅ Fitzpatricks — Zurich Classic, Alex earns PGA Tour card

Up next:
• LIV Virginia: May 8-10, Trump National DC
• PGA Tour: Cadillac Championship

Golf doesn't stop. 🏌️ #PGATour #LIVGolf #LPGA

facebook.long_form:
Window: 8:30 PM ET

What a weekend in the golf world. Korda wins a major. The Fitzpatrick brothers deliver one of the most dramatic tournament finishes of the year. If you're a golf fan, you earned this Monday morning.

So what's next?

On the PGA Tour, the Cadillac Championship is up next for the men's game. The competitive calendar keeps rolling after back-to-back blockbuster weekends featuring the Chevron Championship and the Zurich Classic.

On the LIV Golf circuit, the next stop is Virginia: May 8-10 at Trump National Golf Club Washington D.C. in Sterling, Virginia. Jon Rahm will arrive after his dominant six-shot victory at Mexico City, hungry for more with his Legion XIII squad. Joaquín Niemann looks to defend his individual title from last year. The Crushers GC will try to defend their team title. Meanwhile, Ripper GC leads the 2026 team standings — the fight for the LIV championship is very much alive.

For the LPGA, Nelly Korda heads into the rest of the major season with three titles on her career résumé and her World No. 1 ranking freshly reclaimed. The women's game has its marquee figure at full dominance.

The golf world never stops moving. This week just reminded us why we love it.

#GolfFanrecap | #PGATour | #LIVGolf | #LPGA

article.headline: What's Next in Golf: LIV Virginia, PGA Tour Calendar and Korda's Ongoing LPGA Dominance
